cards.definition: The study of matter, energy, forces, and motion.
A push or pull that can change an object's motion.
The force that attracts objects towards the Earth.
The force of gravity acting on an object. Measured in newtons (N).
The amount of matter in an object. Measured in kilograms (kg).
The unit used to measure force.
A force that opposes motion between two surfaces in contact.
A type of friction caused by air pushing against a moving object.
Equal and opposite forces that do not change an object's motion.
Forces that are not equal, causing an object to accelerate.
The overall force acting on an object after all forces are combined.
A change in an object's position over time.
The total length of the path travelled.
The straight-line distance and direction from the starting point.
The distance travelled per unit of time.
Speed in a particular direction.
The rate of change of velocity.
A decrease in an object's speed (negative acceleration).
The tendency of an object to resist changes in its motion.
The quantity of motion an object has due to its mass and velocity.
The ability to do work or cause change.
The energy an object has because it is moving.
Stored energy due to an object's position or condition.
Energy stored because an object is above the ground.
Energy stored when an object is stretched or compressed.
Energy stored in food, fuels, and batteries.
Energy associated with the movement of particles.
Energy carried by light waves.
Energy carried by vibrations travelling through a medium.
The movement of energy from one object or place to another.
A change from one form of energy into another.
Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred or transformed.
Energy transferred when a force moves an object.
The rate at which work is done or energy is transferred.
The force acting over a given area.
The amount of mass in a given volume.
The amount of space an object occupies.
The upward force exerted by a fluid on an object.
Another name for the buoyant force.
A substance that can flow, such as a liquid or gas.
A disturbance that transfers energy without transferring matter.
The material through which a wave travels.
A wave where vibrations are perpendicular to the direction of travel.
A wave where vibrations are parallel to the direction of travel.
The distance between two identical points on consecutive waves.
The number of waves passing a point each second (Hz).
The maximum distance a wave moves from its resting position.
The bouncing of a wave off a surface.
The bending of a wave as it enters a different medium.
When a material takes in wave energy instead of reflecting it.
A reflected sound wave.
A region in a longitudinal wave where particles are close together.
A region in a longitudinal wave where particles are spread apart.
